title: You Knew Your Place
summary: Interviewed by Dr. Hasan Kwame Jeffries at Albany State University in March 2013, Sam Mahone recalls being born in 1945 in Americus, Sumter County, Georgia, where the lines of separation were stark, and how as a child he read the wrongness of the system in his parents' downcast eyes and lack of eye contact with white people.

title: The Policy Man Walked Right In
topic: A Family Dependent on White Services
summary: Mahone names his father Bergen Mahon, a basket-factory worker, and his mother Lula Bell, a domestic, and describes the dirt roads, the outdoor toilet he had until his senior year, and the white insurance agent who would walk into Black homes without knocking, the everyday liberties that taught a child to know his place out of fear.

title: The Americus Voters League
summary: Around tenth grade, before any civil rights organization reached Americus, Mahone joined a voter registration project started by local ministers called the Americus Voters League, talking to high school classmates about registration, drawn to it as something different to do in a small town with few outlets.

title: Disengaged but Not Against It
summary: Mahone describes his working father's silence and his mother's quiet support of his activity, his older brothers already away in the military and a younger sister who left for Florida, and the field jobs he held as a student: picking cotton, pulling peanuts, picking peaches, and cleaning a clothing store.

title: SNCC Came in Late 1962
summary: Mahone recounts SNCC workers Don Harris, John Perdew, and Ralph Allen arriving in Americus in late 1962, dispatched from Albany by project director Charles Sherrod, drawn by demographics where Black residents were half the population on a median income near twelve hundred dollars a year, and the second-class schools where he never received a new book.

title: The SNCC House Became a Hangout
topic: The Freedom House and Black Authors
summary: Mahone describes the SNCC workers with Northern dialects setting up a shotgun freedom house, introducing students to Richard Wright and James Baldwin, and running mimeograph machines to print leaflets, so the house became an after-school hangout where his activism began and he recognized the duality of the system.

title: Voting Is Power
summary: Mahone explains months of door-to-door canvassing in the city and counties, the Freedom School set up to teach people to read and pass the literacy test, and the nonviolence preparation where workers rehearsed curling into a fetal position and absorbing spit and blows for the violence they knew was coming.

title: Eleven of Us Went to the Front
summary: Mahone tells how students, not SNCC workers, pushed to test public accommodations at the downtown movie theater where Black patrons climbed three flights up a dark alley, how eleven of them tried to buy tickets at the front and were jailed for disorderly conduct, and how a second arrest a week later sent four of them to the Sumter County prison farm.

title: Seven Days in the Hole
summary: Mahone describes being sentenced to begin time immediately, refusing road work, and being put with another boy in a four-by-six concrete hole fed bread and water each day, where they kept a hunger strike in total darkness for about seven days until they passed out and were hospitalized, then made to shovel out pools of raw sewage at the Americus treatment plants, the worst job saved for the movement prisoners alone.

title: They Stood By Me
summary: Mahone remembers his father angry and fearful and his mother bringing him things, and how working the streets let people pass by with news of the movement and money, where he picked up smoking, while watching new arrivals brought to the jail each night kept the jailed students going.

title: The Meanest Man in the World
topic: Sheriff Fred Chappell and the Inmates
summary: Mahone describes the cold, racist jailers and Sheriff Fred Chappell, whom King had called the meanest man in the world after being jailed in Americus in 1961 and whom Mahone likens to Jim Clark and Al Lingo, contrasted with the older inmates who praised the students, protected them like big brothers, and became people to organize.

title: Tire Irons at the Hasty House
summary: Released and now committed, Mahone finished high school and joined SNCC, and on the night the Civil Rights Bill was signed in July 1964 he, John Perdew, and Bob Mants of Morehouse tried to order at the Hasty House restaurant, where whites from a service station attacked them with tire irons and baseball bats as they reached their convertible, beating them on the head while one of their group, attacked at the outside restroom, fled and was not seen for two days.

title: A Century-Old Sedition Law
summary: Mahone recounts a 1963 night march from a mass meeting met with dogs and water hoses, after which SNCC workers Don Harris, John Perdew, Ralph Allen and CORE worker Zev Aelony were charged under a century-old sedition law carrying the death penalty, held some eighty-four days until a three-judge federal panel and a legal team including C.B. King of Albany and Donald Hollowell of Atlanta won their release.

title: The Nation Turned Its Eyes
summary: Mahone describes how the possibility of execution stayed on people's minds, the nationwide fundraising and letter-writing campaign from college campuses and legal groups, and the jubilation when the case went to the Supreme Court and the lower-court ruling was upheld, in a courthouse that is now the welcome center holding the movement's memorabilia.

title: The Leesburg Stockade
summary: Mahone explains that as the Americus jails filled, authorities used abandoned camps in Terrell County and the Leesburg, Georgia stockade, where juvenile girls under sixteen were held in an all-concrete building with one toilet for thirty-five to forty girls, no bedding or showers, a guard who threw a rattlesnake into the holding area, and white men who came asking to have fun with the girls; police had hooded the girls into a tractor trailer in the night so parents did not know where they were for over a week, the episode later told by Jet Magazine as the stolen girls.

title: The Maids Union
summary: Mahone describes how domestic workers were fired when their children joined the movement, so they formed the first Maids Union, started by the mother of several of the jailed girls, to ask for better wages, and how the movement organized a national book drive that brought twenty thousand books to set up a library in the former Colored Hospital that had trained Black doctors.

title: Voting in the White Line
summary: Mahone tells of Mary Kate Bell Fikes, the first Black woman to qualify for the Democratic primary in Sumter County running for Justice of the Peace, and how she with Mamie Campbell, Gloria Wise, and Lena Turner tried to vote in the white voting line in 1965, were arrested, and set off a daily deluge of demonstrations, until the women refused a bond the fearful white merchants offered, sent a letter citing the merchants' hypocrisy, and a federal judge dropped the charges and declared Sumter County's segregated voting lines illegal.

title: A Scholarship to Tougaloo
summary: Mahone describes taking a SNCC scholarship in 1966 to attend Tougaloo College in Mississippi, planning to major in sociology, finding Tougaloo at the forefront of civil rights activity and the only Black institution actively protesting the war, and leaving after two years because he spent his time as an off-campus activist and there was more work to be done.

title: The Lawyer Run Out of Town
summary: Mahone tells of Warren Fortson, a white Americus lawyer and school board head whose brother Ben Fortson was Georgia Secretary of State, who defended Roberta Freeman, an early integrator of the white high school, and her boyfriend on charges Mahone believed were meant to stop her graduating, and who was hounded out of Americus by the Klan, later steering Mahone toward a job in Atlanta.

title: Ancestral Arts Gallery
summary: Mahone recounts taking a job as an apprentice in paper conservation at the Georgia Department of Archives for some twelve to fifteen years, studying photography, and opening Ancestral Arts Gallery, which he ran full time as another form of activism elevating African American artists who lacked venues in Atlanta and hosting poetry sessions and community meetings.

title: A Holdover Company
summary: Mahone describes being drafted in 1969 despite his anti-war protests, kept at Fort Benning after basic training and denied a security clearance over his Americus arrest record, assigned to a holdover company of duty work digging ditches and building an archery course alongside men given a choice between prison and the war, then sent to a material command in Germany where, idle in the back of an office, he first picked up a camera and spent weekends driving to Paris until his release in New Jersey in 1971.

title: We Convened on Americus Again
summary: Mahone explains how in 2007 he and other Americus movement veterans, realizing the history was being lost and unshared with their children, gathered at the Bottoms funeral home whose family had been the movement's bail bondsman, and resolved to hold a tribute every two years and one day build a museum to house the photographs, flyers, and Voice of Americus newsletter they had begun to collect.

title: That's Me in Her Belly
summary: Mahone describes reproducing news articles and photographs from the Atlanta Journal, Columbus Ledger-Enquirer, and Macon Telegraph that the Americus Times-Recorder never reported, displaying them at each celebration, and a young woman who recognized her pregnant mother in a photograph and learned for the first time that her mother had been a foot soldier of the movement.

title: They Stand on Shoulders
summary: Mahone closes on the mission's purpose in Jimmy Carter's hometown: scholarships to nurture a new generation of young activists who must understand they stand on the shoulders of those who made tremendous sacrifices, so that the work continues only if they take the lead, a charge passed to them every two years.
